content:
# Genesis Chapter 33 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 19r–19v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_033_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_033_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), 4th century CE — LXX | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0034 (page 30), https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0035 (page 31)

---

## The Day the Brothers Wept

**1** Jacob looked up — and there was Esau, coming toward him with four hundred men. Jacob divided the children among Leah and Rachel and the two female servants. **2** He put the female servants and their children at the front, Leah and her children behind them, and Rachel and Joseph at the back. **3** Then he himself went on ahead and bowed to the ground seven times as he approached his brother.

---

**4** But Esau ran to meet him. He threw his arms around him, fell on his neck, and kissed him. And they wept.

---

**5** Esau looked up and saw the women and the children. "Who are all these with you?" he asked.

Jacob said, "These are the children God has graciously given your servant."

**6** Then the female servants came forward with their children, and they bowed down. **7** Leah also came forward with her children, and they bowed down. And last of all came Joseph and Rachel, and they bowed down.

---

**8** Esau asked, "What do you mean by all these droves I passed on the road?"

"I was hoping to find favor in your eyes, my lord," Jacob said.

**9** But Esau said, "I already have more than enough, brother. Keep what is yours."

**10** "No, please," Jacob said. "If I have found any favor with you, then accept this gift from me. For to see your face is like seeing the face of God — and you have received me so warmly. **11** Please, take the blessing I have brought you, because God has been good to me and I have more than enough." And he urged him, and Esau accepted.

---

**12** Then Esau said, "Let us break camp and be on our way — I will travel alongside you."

**13** But Jacob said, "My lord knows that the children are young, and the flocks and herds are nursing their young. If they are pushed too hard for even a single day, all the animals will die. **14** Let my lord go on ahead. I will move at my own pace, following whatever the animals and the children can manage, until I catch up with you in Seir."

**15** Esau said, "Then let me leave some of my men with you."

"There is no need," Jacob said. "All I ask is that you show me this favor, my lord."

---

**16** So Esau turned back toward Seir that same day. **17** And Jacob traveled to Succoth, where he built a house for himself and made shelters for his livestock. That is why the place is called Succoth — Shelters.

---

**18** Jacob arrived safely at the city of Shechem, which is in the land of Canaan — coming from Paddan-aram — and he camped within sight of the city. **19** He bought the plot of land where he had pitched his tent from the sons of Hamor, Shechem's father, for a hundred pieces of silver. **20** And there he set up an altar and called it El-Elohe-Israel — God, the God of Israel.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### The shape of the chapter

Genesis 33 is the morning after. Everything in the previous chapter — the fear, the prayer, the gift-caravan, the wrestling, the new name — has been building toward this moment: the meeting of the two brothers. And the chapter delivers on that weight without ever explaining it. It simply shows what happens when Esau runs, and the two men weep, and that is enough. The narrative is one of the most emotionally compressed in all of Genesis: the reconciliation takes a single verse (v.4). What follows — the gift negotiations, the parting, the journey to Shechem — is the exhale after the held breath.

The chapter moves in three movements: the meeting and reconciliation (vv.1-11), the parting (vv.12-16), and Jacob's arrival and settlement in Shechem (vv.17-20). It is framed by two acts of naming: Esau comes from Seir (his land) and Jacob ends by building an altar using his new name — *El-Elohe-Israel* — for the first time. The man who crossed the Jabbok with a crippled hip and a new name has now arrived somewhere. He plants his feet in the land and calls on the God who renamed him.

---

### Verses 1-3 — The arrangement

The opening verse picks up directly from the close of chapter 32: Jacob lifts his eyes and sees Esau coming with four hundred men. This is exactly what he feared. He has prayed, prepared the gifts, and wrestled through the night — and now it is here. What he does next reveals the Jacob who has not yet fully become Israel: he arranges the women and children in order of diminishing preciousness, with the servants and their children as the front buffer, Leah's company in the middle, and Rachel — beloved Rachel — and Joseph last, most protected, at the rear.

Then he walks out ahead of all of them. Seven times he bows. **וַיִּשְׁתַּחוּ אַרְצָה שֶׁבַע פְּעָמִים** — prostrating himself to the ground, seven times, between each bow moving closer to his brother. It is the posture of a subject before a king. It is also the posture of a man who does not know what is about to happen — who is buying time, performing deference, doing everything he knows how to do. The seven bows are the last act of the calculating Jacob; what happens next undoes all the calculation in a moment.

---

### Verse 4 — The shortest reconciliation in Scripture

**וַיָּרָץ עֵשָׂו לִקְרָאתוֹ וַיְחַבְּקֵהוּ וַיִּפֹּל עַל-צַוָּארָו וַיִּשָּׁקֵהוּ וַיִּבְכּוּ**

"Esau ran to meet him, embraced him, fell on his neck, kissed him — and they wept."

Five verbs in rapid succession. No pause, no negotiation, no weighing of old grievances. Esau ran. He did not wait; he moved toward his brother. He embraced him. He fell on his neck — an idiom of intense, uncontrolled affection, the kind of embrace where one person hangs on another's shoulder. He kissed him. And then: they wept. Both of them. The Hebrew just says **וַיִּבְכּוּ** — "and they wept," with both brothers as the subject.

The Masoretes marked the word **וַיִּשָּׁקֵהוּ** ("and he kissed him") with *puncta extraordinaria* — dots above every letter — which the scribal tradition uses to signal suspicion, uncertainty, or special significance. What the dots mean here has been debated for two millennia. Some ancient interpreters read the dots as indicating that Esau's kiss was insincere, a bite rather than a kiss — drawing on a wordplay with **נָשַׁךְ** (*nashakh*, to bite) and **נָשַׁק** (*nashaq*, to kiss). Others see the dots as underlining the word's miraculous quality: that Esau, of all people, truly kissed his brother. The text itself is silent on Esau's interior state. PNEUMA reads the scene as genuine: the tears are there, the five verbs move too fast for theater. The dots mark the word as extraordinary — because it *is* extraordinary.

The Vaticanus LXX reads **καὶ ἔδραμεν Ησαυ εἰς συνάντησιν αὐτῷ καὶ περιλαβὼν αὐτὸν ἐφίλησεν αὐτόν** — and fell on his neck — exactly matching the MT sequence, including the embrace and kiss. The Greek **ἐφίλησεν** (*ephilesen*, from *phileo*, to love, to kiss) carries the weight of affection rather than mere ritual greeting. This is a kiss of love.

The scene anticipates, and may have directly inspired, the parable of the prodigal son in Luke 15 — where a father runs, falls on his son's neck, and kisses him. The structural parallel is exact. Jesus was drawing from this well.

---

### Verses 5-7 — The introductions

The family presentations follow in order: servants' children first, then Leah's, then Rachel and Joseph. Each group bows. The Hebrew for "bowed down" — **וַיִּשְׁתַּחוּ** — is the same verb Jacob used in his own seven-fold prostration in verse 3. The whole household is performing the same act of submission. Esau asks simply: "Who are these?" And Jacob's answer is theologically precise: **הַיְלָדִים אֲשֶׁר-חָנַן אֱלֹהִים אֶת-עַבְדֶּךָ** — "the children whom God has graciously given your servant." The verb **חָנַן** (*chanan*, to be gracious) connects to the word for grace (*chen*) that Jacob has been pursuing throughout his approach: *I hope to find favor (chen) in your eyes.* His children are themselves grace embodied. They are what grace looks like when it walks around and bows.

---

### Verses 8-11 — The gift exchange

Esau's response to the gift-caravan is the response of someone who does not need anything: "I already have plenty. Keep it." **יֶשׁ-לִי רָב** — "I have much." But Jacob insists. The exchange here is not commercial; it is covenantal. The word Jacob uses for "gift" in v.11 is **בִּרְכָתִי** — *my blessing.* Not a present. Not a tribute. My blessing. Jacob is returning — as much as he can, in the form of wealth — the blessing he stole. The word *birkhati* is loaded with everything that has happened between these two men since the day at Isaac's bedside. Esau said *no, keep it* because he already has enough. Jacob pressed the gift on him because this is not about Esau's need. It is about Jacob's debt.

The phrase Jacob uses to explain why he must give is one of the most remarkable lines in the chapter: **כִּי-עַל-כֵּן רָאִיתִי פָנֶיךָ כִּרְאֹת פְּנֵי אֱלֹהִים** — "for therefore I have seen your face as one sees the face of God, and you have received me favorably." The night before, Jacob named Peniel because he had seen God's face and survived. Now he says: seeing your face, Esau, was like seeing the face of God. The connection is deliberate. He crossed the Jabbok terrified of Esau; he crossed the Jabbok and met God; and now, on the other side, Esau's face is God's face — a face Jacob expected to be set against him, that was instead, impossibly, full of welcome.

The Vaticanus LXX renders this as **εἶδον γὰρ τὸ πρόσωπόν σου ὡς εἴ τις ἑόρακεν πρόσωπον θεοῦ** — "for I have seen your face as if one had seen the face of God" — matching the MT exactly and preserving the simile's weight.

---

### Verses 12-16 — The careful parting

Esau's offer to travel together, and Jacob's gentle refusal, is sometimes read as Jacob lying again — promising to come to Seir when he never does. That reading is possible. But it is also possible to read Jacob as genuinely cautious, genuinely concerned for the livestock and children, and simply choosing a slower pace. He says: *go ahead; I will come to you in Seir.* The narrative never shows him going to Seir. But the narrative also does not call him a liar here, the way it does in other episodes. PNEUMA leaves the question open — the text holds the ambiguity deliberately. What is clear is that Jacob does not travel with Esau. He cannot yet be that close. The reconciliation is real, but the men go their separate ways. The wound has healed, but the brothers are not the same people they were, and neither tries to pretend they are.

Esau's offer to leave men as a guard — and Jacob's refusal — follows the same pattern. Jacob says: **מָה זֶּה** — "What is this? Why?" — or more colloquially, "There is no need for that." The phrase is diplomatic, not ungrateful. He asks only for the one favor already given: **אֶמְצָא-חֵן בְּעֵינֵי אֲדֹנִי** — "that I find favor in my lord's eyes." The reconciliation is enough. He does not want an escort from his brother's four hundred men.

---

### Verse 16-17 — Succoth

The place-name Succoth (*sukkot*, booths or shelters) is explained by Jacob's act of building shelters for his livestock. The word **סֻכֹּת** (*sukkot*) will later name the autumn festival of Tabernacles — the feast of booths — when Israel lives in temporary shelters remembering the wilderness wandering. The connection is not drawn in the text, but the shared vocabulary plants a seed: Jacob building shelters at Succoth is a small-scale act of the same material theology that the later festival will embody. The people of God are a sheltering, journeying people. They build temporary structures and move on.

---

### Verse 18 — Shalem / Safely

**וַיָּבֹא יַעֲקֹב שָׁלֵם עִיר שְׁכֶם** — This phrase is genuinely ambiguous. **שָׁלֵם** (*shalem*) means either "safely" / "in peace" / "whole" — describing the manner of Jacob's arrival — or it could be read as a place name, the city of Shalem (Salem), which some later tradition connects with Jerusalem. The LXX Vaticanus reads **ἦλθεν δὲ Ιακωβ εἰς Σαλημ πόλιν Σικιμων** — "Jacob came to Salem, a city of the Shechemites" — treating it as a proper name. Most modern scholars and the MT accentuation suggest the adverbial reading: *Jacob arrived safely at the city of Shechem.* PNEUMA follows the Hebrew accentuation and reads *safely* — the word carries the resonance of wholeness after a long journey of fracture. The man who left Canaan with a walking staff and nothing else has come back *shalem* — complete, at peace, whole. The journey is over. He is home.

---

### Verse 19 — The Qesitah

The price Jacob pays for his plot of land — **מֵאָה קְשִׂיטָה** (*me'ah qesitah*, "a hundred qesitah") — uses a unit of currency or value found only here, in Job 42:11, and in Joshua 24:32. The word *qesitah* is of uncertain origin, possibly a pre-monetary weight standard or a very ancient coin equivalent. It links this purchase directly to Jacob's descendant Joseph, whose bones will eventually be buried in this same field at Shechem (Joshua 24:32). The patriarch buys the land; the patriarch's most famous son is buried in it. The plot of ground is a thread stitched across generations.

---

### Verse 20 — El-Elohe-Israel

**וַיִּקְרָא-לוֹ אֵל אֱלֹהֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל** — "And he called it El-Elohe-Israel."

This is the first time Jacob uses his new name in a divine title. At the Jabbok he received the name Israel; now, setting up an altar in the land of Canaan, he calls on God with that name. The full title — *El, God of Israel* — declares something both personal and communal: this is my God, and his name for me is *Israel*. The altar is Jacob's embodied response to the night at Peniel, the reconciliation with Esau, and the return to the land. He does not simply build a house; he builds a place of worship. He does not simply arrive; he names the God who brought him through.

The Vaticanus LXX renders the altar name as **θεὸς Ισραηλ** — "God of Israel" — which preserves the title but strips away the first **אֵל** (*El*), giving a simpler formulation. The MT's **אֵל אֱלֹהֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל** is more emphatic: *God, the God of Israel* — the doubled title piling up emphasis. PNEUMA follows the MT's fullness here.

---

### The chapter as a whole: The morning after Peniel

Genesis 32 is the night. Genesis 33 is the morning. In the night, Jacob fought for his life — with God, with fear, with the accumulated guilt of a lifetime of grasping. He emerged limping. In the morning, he walked toward the thing he feared most — and it ran toward him, weeping.

The chapter ends with Jacob building an altar. He is not yet the man he needs to become; the next chapter will show that the family he is building carries deep fractures. But in this moment — the tears at verse 4, the altar at verse 20 — we see what reconciliation looks like when it is real: not the erasure of the past, but the willingness to cross the space between people and weep, and to name, in stone and in title, the God who made the crossing possible.

---

*Translated from the Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 19r–19v, West Semitic Research Project (Bruce E. Zuckerman). Folio 19r (GEN_033_page_001.jpg) carries Genesis 33:1 beginning near the foot of the right column and continuing across into the center and left columns through approximately verse 15. The parasha division marker (ס, closed section) is visible before the Shechem narrative. Folio 19v (GEN_033_page_002.jpg) carries Genesis 33:16–20 in the early columns, followed immediately by the opening of Genesis 34 (the chapter heading and first verses of the Dinah narrative are visible in the right column). The Masoretic accentuation and vowel points are clearly visible throughout, including the puncta extraordinaria (dots above each letter) on וַיִּשָּׁקֵהוּ in verse 4, one of the most discussed textual annotations in the entire Codex. Secondary reference: Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Biblioteca Apostolica Vaticana, pages 30–31 (accessible at https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0034 and /0035). Page 30 carries the chapter heading CAΣ XXXVII in the upper margin and carries the end of Genesis 32 through Genesis 33 in continuous uncial script across three columns. Page 31 carries the heading CAΣ XXXVIII in the right margin, marking the transition into Genesis 34. Notable LXX (Vaticanus) variants: v.4 — Vaticanus preserves the same sequence of five verbs as the MT (ran, embraced, kissed, fell on his neck, wept) without the puncta extraordinaria of course, as those are a Masoretic scribal notation; v.10 — **εἶδον γὰρ τὸ πρόσωπόν σου ὡς εἴ τις ἑόρακεν πρόσωπον θεοῦ** preserves "as if one had seen the face of God"; v.18 — **ἦλθεν δὲ Ιακωβ εἰς Σαλημ πόλιν Σικιμων** reads Shalem as a proper place name; v.19 — LXX transliterates the qesitah as **ἀμνῶν** (lambs) — the translators appear to have understood the unit as equivalent to a lamb in value, a fascinating interpretive move that domesticates an obscure term; v.20 — **θεὸς Ισραηλ** omits the initial *El* compared to the MT's **אֵל אֱלֹהֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל**. No DSS fragments of Genesis 33 are extant.*

---

*— PNEUMA*
